tagline: Windows commands for people who have work to do.

commands.publicCommand: Show what Windows knows about me
aliases: what does Microsoft have on me
what is Windows tracking about me
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Displays the personal data, telemetry settings, and diagnostic information Windows has collected or stored about you and your usage habits.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Reading your Windows privacy data. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is what Windows has been storing about you. Review the results to decide what you want to turn off.

commands.publicCommand: Stop Windows from sending my data to Microsoft
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables telemetry, diagnostic data uploads, and other background reporting that sends your usage data to Microsoft servers.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable Windows telemetry and data reporting to Microsoft. Some Microsoft services and Windows Update troubleshooting may be affected.
commands.afterRunMessage: Windows telemetry and data reporting have been disabled. Your PC will no longer automatically send usage data to Microsoft.

aliases: stop Windows selling my data to advertisers
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Resets and disables your Windows advertising ID so apps and Microsoft cannot build a profile of you for targeted advertising.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable your Windows advertising ID. Apps will no longer be able to serve you personalized ads based on your usage.
commands.afterRunMessage: Ad targeting has been disabled. Your advertising ID has been reset and switched off.

aliases: stop Windows knowing where I am
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Turns off Windows location services so apps and the operating system cannot determine or log your physical location.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable Windows location services. Apps that rely on your location such as Maps and Weather will stop working correctly.
commands.afterRunMessage: Location tracking has been disabled. Apps can no longer access your physical location through Windows.

commands.publicCommand: See which apps know my location
aliases: which apps can see where I am
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists every app that has been granted permission to access your location, along with when they last used it.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking which apps have location access. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are all the apps that can see your location. You can revoke access for any app you do not trust.

commands.publicCommand: See which apps have used my microphone
aliases: what apps have been recording audio
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Shows every app that has microphone permission and which ones have recently accessed your microphone.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking microphone access history. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are all the apps with microphone access. If you see anything unexpected, you can revoke its permission.

commands.publicCommand: See which apps have used my camera
aliases: what apps have accessed my webcam
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Shows every app that has camera permission and which ones have recently accessed your webcam or camera.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking camera access history. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are all the apps with camera access. Review the list and revoke access for anything that should not be watching.

commands.publicCommand: Stop Cortana from listening and collecting data
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables Cortana's background listening, data collection, and cloud search features so it stops processing your voice and queries.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable Cortana's listening and data collection. Cortana voice features and some search integrations will stop working.
commands.afterRunMessage: Cortana has been silenced. It is no longer listening or sending your data to Microsoft.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Clears your Windows activity history and Timeline data including apps used, files opened, and websites visited through Edge.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will permanently delete your Windows activity history. Your Timeline and recent activity will be cleared.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your activity history has been cleared. Windows no longer has a log of your recent actions.

commands.publicCommand: Stop Windows from collecting my typing data
aliases: stop Windows watching what I type
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables the inking and typing personalization feature that sends samples of what you type to Microsoft to improve autocorrect and suggestions.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable Windows typing data collection. Autocomplete suggestions may become less accurate over time.
commands.afterRunMessage: Windows will no longer collect your typing data. Keystroke personalization has been disabled.

commands.publicCommand: Hide my PC from network discovery
aliases: make my PC invisible on the network
make my computer private on WiFi
stop being visible on local network
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables network discovery so other computers and devices on the same network cannot see your PC in their file explorer or network browser.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will hide your PC from other devices on the network. File and printer sharing with other computers will also be affected.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your PC is now hidden from network discovery. Other devices on the same network can no longer browse to your computer.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Clears the local and cloud search history that Windows stores, removing autocomplete suggestions and recent search entries from the Start menu search bar.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will clear your Windows search history. Search suggestions based on your past searches will be removed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your Windows search history has been cleared. The search bar will no longer suggest things from your past searches.

commands.publicCommand: Remove the news feed and widgets from my taskbar
aliases: get rid of news on taskbar
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Removes the widgets panel and news feed from your taskbar, which also stops Microsoft from tracking your news reading habits and interests.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will remove the widgets button and news feed from your taskbar. The widgets panel will no longer appear.
commands.afterRunMessage: Widgets and the news feed have been removed from your taskbar. Microsoft can no longer track your reading habits through this feed.

commands.publicCommand: Strip Edge of its tracking and sidebar
aliases: turn off Edge shopping and tracking
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables Edge's shopping assistant, sidebar, tracking features, and telemetry so the browser focuses on browsing rather than collecting your data.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable Edge tracking, the sidebar, shopping suggestions, and telemetry. These features will no longer appear in Edge.
commands.afterRunMessage: Edge has been stripped down. Tracking, the sidebar, and shopping features have been disabled.

commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables visual animations, adjusts visual effect settings for best performance, and tweaks system responsiveness so Windows feels noticeably snappier.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable Windows animations and visual effects. Your PC will look more basic but will respond much faster.
commands.afterRunMessage: Windows has been tuned for speed. Animations are off and visual effects are set for best performance.

aliases: why does startup take so long
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Identifies and disables programs that launch automatically at startup and are slowing down your boot time.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable startup programs that are slowing your boot. Disabled programs will still be usable — they just will not start automatically.
commands.afterRunMessage: Startup programs have been trimmed. Your next boot should be noticeably faster.

commands.publicCommand: Stop apps running in the background
aliases: apps running when I am not using them
stop apps I am not using
why is my CPU busy when idle
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Prevents apps from running in the background when you are not actively using them, freeing up CPU, RAM, and battery.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will prevent apps from running in the background. Notifications and live tile updates from these apps may stop working.
commands.afterRunMessage: Background app activity has been restricted. Apps will only run when you are actively using them.

aliases: PC is freezing because of RAM
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Clears the Windows standby memory list and flushes cached data from RAM to immediately free up available memory without rebooting.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will free up RAM by clearing cached data. It is safe and you will not lose any open work.
commands.afterRunMessage: RAM has been freed up. Your system should feel more responsive right away.

commands.publicCommand: Find what is maxing out my CPU
aliases: what is using all my CPU
PC running hot for no reason
find the process killing my CPU
what is maxing out my processor
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Identifies which processes and applications are consuming the most CPU right now and shows you their resource usage over time.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Scanning for CPU-hungry processes. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are the processes using the most CPU. You can use this information to decide what to close or disable.

commands.publicCommand: Stop search indexing from slowing my drive
aliases: search indexing is killing my drive
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables or limits the Windows Search indexing service that constantly reads and writes to your drive in the background.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable Windows Search indexing. File searches from the Start menu may be slower, but your drive will have much less background activity.
commands.afterRunMessage: Search indexing has been disabled. Your drive should be quieter and your PC more responsive during idle periods.

commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Switches your power plan to High Performance or Ultimate Performance so Windows stops throttling your CPU and other hardware to save power.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will switch to a high performance power plan. Your PC will run faster but use more electricity. Not recommended for laptops on battery.
commands.afterRunMessage: High performance mode is active. Your CPU and hardware are no longer being throttled.

aliases: why is my disk always at 100 percent
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables the SysMain service (formerly Superfetch) that pre-loads apps into RAM in the background, which can cause constant disk thrashing especially on older drives.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable SysMain. App launch pre-loading will stop, but disk activity will decrease significantly. Recommended for HDDs and low-RAM systems.
commands.afterRunMessage: SysMain has been disabled. Background disk thrashing should stop. Your drive will be much quieter during idle.

commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Deletes temporary files from the Windows temp folders, user temp folder, and prefetch cache that accumulate over time and waste space.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will delete temporary files. Any open apps that are using temp files may be affected. It is safe to run while most apps are closed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Temporary files have been deleted. Check how much space was recovered in the results above.

commands.publicCommand: Show what is using my RAM
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Displays a sorted list of every process and application currently consuming RAM so you can identify what is taking up the most memory.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking RAM usage across all running processes. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is your RAM breakdown. The biggest consumers are at the top. Consider closing apps you do not need.

commands.publicCommand: Show everything that starts with Windows
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists all programs, services, and scheduled tasks that automatically start when Windows boots, including their startup impact rating.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Scanning your startup configuration. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is everything that starts with Windows. Use this list to decide what does not need to run on every boot.

commands.publicCommand: Check how fast my PC is right now
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Runs a quick performance snapshot showing CPU, RAM, disk, and network speeds alongside current load, so you know how your hardware is actually performing right now.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Running a quick performance snapshot. This will take a few seconds and will not change anything.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are your current performance numbers. Compare them against expected speeds for your hardware to spot bottlenecks.

commands.publicCommand: Show what runs automatically on a schedule
aliases: what is Windows running behind my back
what does Windows do on a schedule
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists all Windows scheduled tasks that run automatically at defined times or triggers, including Microsoft and third-party tasks.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Retrieving all scheduled tasks. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are all the tasks Windows runs on a schedule. Look for anything unfamiliar or tasks from apps you no longer use.

commands.publicCommand: Show where my disk space went
aliases: find what is using my storage
what is taking up all my space
commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Scans your drives and shows a breakdown of what is consuming your disk space, organized by folder and file type.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Scanning your drive for space usage. This may take a moment on large drives. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is where your disk space went. Focus on the largest items to recover the most space.

commands.publicCommand: Find the largest files on my PC
aliases: what is taking the most space
commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Searches your drive and returns a ranked list of the largest files so you can quickly identify candidates to delete or move.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Searching for the largest files on your drive. This may take a few minutes. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are your biggest files. Delete or move any you no longer need to recover significant space.

commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Uses Disk Cleanup and DISM to remove superseded Windows update files, temporary update caches, and component store backups that are no longer needed.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will permanently delete old Windows update files. You will not be able to uninstall those specific updates afterward, but your current Windows version will not be affected.
commands.afterRunMessage: Old Windows update files have been removed. Check the results to see how much space was recovered.

commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Scans your folders for files with identical content and lists the duplicates so you can review and delete the extras.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Scanning for duplicate files. This may take a while depending on how many files you have. Nothing will be deleted yet.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are your duplicate files. Review the list carefully and delete only the copies you are sure you do not need.

commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Permanently removes the Windows.old folder that was left behind after a Windows upgrade, recovering the disk space it occupies.
commands.beforeRunMessage: WARNING: Windows.old will be permanently deleted. You will no longer be able to roll back to the previous Windows version. This cannot be undone.
commands.afterRunMessage: Windows.old has been deleted. The disk space has been recovered. You can no longer roll back to the previous version of Windows.

commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Reduces the size of the hiberfil.sys file that Windows uses for hibernation, or disables hibernation entirely to reclaim the space it uses.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will shrink or disable the hibernation file. If hibernation is fully disabled, the hibernate option will be removed from the power menu.
commands.afterRunMessage: The hibernation file has been adjusted. Check how much space was recovered.

commands.publicCommand: Find files apps downloaded without asking
commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Searches common locations where apps silently download files, caches, and updates without your explicit permission.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Scanning for files downloaded by apps without your knowledge. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are files that apps downloaded without explicitly asking you. Review the list and remove anything you do not want.

commands.publicCommand: Find old installer files I never deleted
aliases: old install files on my drive
commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Locates setup and installer files across your drives that were downloaded to install software but never cleaned up afterward.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Searching for old installer and setup files. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are the installer files found on your PC. If the software is already installed, you can safely delete the installer files.

commands.publicCommand: Move my Downloads folder to another drive
commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Relocates your Downloads folder to a different drive or partition so new downloads no longer fill up your main Windows drive.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will move your Downloads folder to a new location. Existing files will be moved and all future downloads will go to the new location.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your Downloads folder has been moved. Future downloads will go to the new location automatically.

commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Clears cached files, temporary internet files, and other leftover data that browsers store on your drive over time.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will clear browser cache and temporary files for detected browsers. You may need to log back in to some websites afterward.
commands.afterRunMessage: Browser leftover files have been cleared. Some websites may load slightly slower on first visit as they rebuild their cache.

aliases: what password am I connected with
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Retrieves and displays the stored password for your currently connected WiFi network or any saved network you specify.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Retrieving your stored WiFi password. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is your WiFi password. Keep it somewhere safe and do not share it with people you do not trust.

aliases: what WiFi networks does my PC know
see every network I have connected to
what WiFi passwords does Windows have
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists all WiFi network profiles saved on your PC, including networks you have connected to in the past.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Retrieving all saved WiFi network profiles. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are all the WiFi networks your PC has saved. You can forget any networks you no longer use or recognize.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Removes a saved WiFi network profile from Windows so your PC will no longer automatically connect to it or remember its password.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will delete the saved profile for the selected network. You will need to re-enter the password if you want to connect to it again.
commands.afterRunMessage: The network profile has been removed. Your PC will no longer auto-connect to that network.

aliases: what IP does my PC have
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Displays your local network IP address, your public internet IP address, subnet mask, and gateway information.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Retrieving your IP address information. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are your IP addresses. Your local IP is used inside your home network. Your public IP is what websites see.

aliases: why can I not get online
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Runs a series of network repair steps including flushing DNS, resetting the TCP/IP stack, releasing and renewing your IP address, and resetting Winsock.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will reset several network components to fix your connection. Your network may disconnect briefly during the repair process.
commands.afterRunMessage: Network repair steps have been completed. If you are still offline, try restarting your router and then running a full network reset.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Performs a complete network stack reset, removing all network adapters and reinstalling them, and resetting all TCP/IP, DNS, and Winsock settings to defaults.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will completely reset your network configuration. All saved network settings will be wiped and your PC will need to reboot. Re-enter your WiFi password after restart.
commands.afterRunMessage: Network reset is complete. Your PC needs to restart to finish the process. After reboot, reconnect to your WiFi network.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Changes your DNS server from your ISP's default to a faster and more privacy-friendly option such as Cloudflare 1.1.1.1 or Google 8.8.8.8.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will change your DNS server. Websites may load faster or resolve differently. If anything breaks, you can always switch back.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your DNS has been changed. Websites should resolve faster. Run a speed test to confirm the improvement.

commands.publicCommand: See who is on my WiFi
aliases: see everything connected to my router
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Scans your local network and lists all devices currently connected, showing their IP address, hostname, and MAC address where available.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Scanning your local network for connected devices. This may take up to 30 seconds. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are all devices found on your network. If you see anything unfamiliar, change your WiFi password and consider enabling MAC filtering.

commands.publicCommand: Stop automatic downloads on this connection
aliases: stop Windows downloading on this network
stop data usage on this network
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Marks your current network connection as metered, which tells Windows and apps to minimize background data usage and pause automatic updates.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will mark your connection as metered. Windows updates and some app syncing will pause or reduce data usage on this network.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your connection is now set as metered. Windows will reduce background downloads and automatic updates on this network.

commands.publicCommand: Show which apps are using my internet
aliases: which apps are online right now
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Shows a breakdown of which apps and processes are currently using your internet connection and how much data each has consumed.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking network usage by application. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is your app-by-app internet usage. Anything consuming unexpected amounts of data is worth investigating.

commands.publicCommand: Block an app from the internet
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Creates a Windows Firewall rule to block a specific application from making any outbound or inbound internet connections.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will create a firewall rule that blocks the selected app from the internet. The app will still run but will not be able to connect online.
commands.afterRunMessage: The firewall rule has been created. The selected app is now blocked from the internet. You can remove the rule at any time to restore access.

aliases: what is my PC connected to
what servers am I talking to
what is my PC communicating with
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists all active TCP and UDP network connections, the ports they use, and the remote addresses your PC is currently communicating with.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Retrieving all active network connections. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are all active connections. Look for any unfamiliar remote addresses or ports that could indicate unwanted background communication.

commands.publicCommand: Stop my PC from waking up on its own
aliases: PC keeps waking up at night
why does my PC wake up
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Identifies and disables the wake timers, scheduled tasks, and devices that are causing your PC to wake from sleep on its own.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable wake timers and adjust device settings that are waking your PC. Some scheduled maintenance tasks may no longer run at night.
commands.afterRunMessage: Wake timers have been disabled. Your PC should stay asleep until you decide to wake it.

commands.publicCommand: Find out what woke my computer
aliases: why did my computer turn on
why did my PC come on
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Reads the Windows event log to show exactly what triggered your PC to wake from sleep or hibernation.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking the event log for wake events. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is what woke your PC. Use this information to identify and disable the culprit.

aliases: how much battery life is left
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Generates a detailed battery health report showing your battery's design capacity, current capacity, and how much it has degraded over time.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Generating your battery health report. This will take a few seconds. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your battery report is ready. Check the design capacity vs current capacity to see how much your battery has worn down.

aliases: how do I get more battery
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Applies power saving settings including reduced screen brightness, stricter background app limits, and a balanced or power saver plan to maximize battery runtime.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will apply battery-saving settings. Your screen may dim and some background features may be limited to extend battery life.
commands.afterRunMessage: Battery saving settings have been applied. Your laptop should last longer on a charge.

aliases: PC not waking from sleep properly
computer does not wake up correctly
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables Fast Startup which can cause wake and resume failures, and resets related power settings to fix common sleep and wake problems.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable Fast Startup and adjust sleep-related settings. Your PC may take slightly longer to boot but should wake from sleep reliably.
commands.afterRunMessage: Fast Startup has been disabled and sleep settings have been reset. Test your sleep and wake behavior to confirm the fix.

commands.publicCommand: See which apps are draining my battery
aliases: what is draining my laptop battery
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Shows a breakdown of battery usage by app over the past 24 hours and 7 days so you can identify which applications are consuming the most power.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking battery usage history by app. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is your battery usage by app. The top drainers are listed first. Consider limiting or closing apps that are using more power than expected.

commands.publicCommand: Stop USB devices from waking my PC
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables the wake-on-signal capability for USB devices so that mice, keyboards, and other USB peripherals can no longer wake your PC from sleep.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will prevent USB devices from waking your PC. You will need to press the power button to wake your PC from sleep instead of clicking the mouse.
commands.afterRunMessage: USB wake has been disabled. Your USB devices can no longer wake the PC from sleep.

commands.publicCommand: Change how long before screen turns off
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Sets the amount of idle time before Windows turns off your display, separately for when plugged in and when on battery.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will change your screen timeout setting. Your display will turn off after the new time period of inactivity.
commands.afterRunMessage: Screen timeout has been updated. Your display will now follow the new timing you set.

commands.bookChapter: Your Passwords Are in Here Somewhere
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Opens and reads the Windows Credential Manager to display saved passwords, usernames, and credentials stored by Windows and connected apps.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Retrieving saved credentials from Windows Credential Manager. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are the credentials Windows has saved. Review the list and remove any outdated or unrecognized entries.

commands.publicCommand: See who has logged into this PC
commands.bookChapter: Your Passwords Are in Here Somewhere
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Reads the Windows Security event log to show a history of successful and failed login attempts on this PC, including account names and timestamps.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Reading the Windows login event log. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is your login history. Look for any logins at unusual times or from accounts you do not recognize.

commands.bookChapter: Your Passwords Are in Here Somewhere
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Triggers a full Windows Defender offline scan that can detect rootkits and malware that evade normal scans by running before Windows fully loads.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will schedule a full offline malware scan. Your PC will need to restart to run the scan before Windows loads. Save all open work before continuing.
commands.afterRunMessage: The offline scan has been scheduled. Your PC will restart and run the deep scan automatically. Results will appear after Windows reloads.

commands.publicCommand: Check if my drive is encrypted
aliases: is my data safe if PC is stolen
commands.bookChapter: Your Passwords Are in Here Somewhere
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Checks whether BitLocker drive encryption is enabled on your drives and shows the encryption status and protection state for each volume.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking BitLocker encryption status. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is the encryption status for your drives. If BitLocker is off, your data could be read by anyone with physical access to your drive.

commands.publicCommand: Back up my BitLocker recovery key
aliases: copy recovery key to safe place
commands.bookChapter: Your Passwords Are in Here Somewhere
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Retrieves your BitLocker recovery key and guides you through saving it to a safe location such as a file, printer, or Microsoft account.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will retrieve your BitLocker recovery key. Store it somewhere secure and separate from your PC — you will need it if you ever cannot boot.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your BitLocker recovery key has been displayed. Make sure you save it somewhere safe outside of this PC before closing this window.

commands.publicCommand: See what permissions my apps have
aliases: what do installed apps have access to
commands.bookChapter: Your Passwords Are in Here Somewhere
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists the sensitive permissions granted to your installed apps including camera, microphone, location, contacts, and calendar access.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Auditing app permissions. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are the permissions your apps have been granted. Revoke access for any app that does not need a particular permission.

aliases: is there malware in my startup
commands.bookChapter: Your Passwords Are in Here Somewhere
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Scans startup locations including registry run keys, scheduled tasks, and startup folders for entries that look suspicious or unusual.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Scanning startup locations for suspicious entries. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are your startup entries with risk indicators. Investigate any entries with unknown publishers, unusual paths, or obfuscated names.

commands.publicCommand: See who has admin rights on this PC
aliases: who has full control of this PC
commands.bookChapter: Your Passwords Are in Here Somewhere
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists all user accounts on this PC that have administrator privileges so you can verify only trusted accounts have full control.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking which accounts have administrator rights. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are all accounts with administrator access. If you see any accounts that should not have admin rights, remove them from the administrators group.

aliases: remove a saved username and password
commands.bookChapter: Your Passwords Are in Here Somewhere
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Removes a specific saved password or credential from the Windows Credential Manager so it is no longer stored on this PC.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will permanently delete the selected saved credential. You will need to re-enter the password manually next time you access that resource.
commands.afterRunMessage: The saved credential has been removed. Windows will ask you to log in manually next time you access that resource.

commands.publicCommand: Check if my firewall is on
commands.bookChapter: Your Passwords Are in Here Somewhere
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Checks the status of Windows Defender Firewall across all network profiles and reports whether it is enabled and protecting your connection.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking Windows Firewall status. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is your firewall status for each network profile. If any profile shows as disabled, re-enable it immediately.

commands.publicCommand: Show everything installed on my PC
aliases: what is installed on my PC
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Generates a complete list of all installed applications including traditional desktop apps, Microsoft Store apps, and system components with their version and install date.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Retrieving a full list of installed software. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here is everything installed on your PC. Look for apps you do not recognize or no longer need.

aliases: get rid of factory installed apps
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Identifies and removes common manufacturer-installed bloatware from brands like Dell, HP, Lenovo, and Asus that came pre-loaded on your PC.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will uninstall identified manufacturer bloatware. Review the list carefully — some manufacturer apps like driver utilities may be useful. Removed apps can be reinstalled from the manufacturer website.
commands.afterRunMessage: Manufacturer bloatware has been removed. Your PC should have more storage and fewer background processes.

aliases: delete apps I never asked for
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Removes Microsoft-installed apps that come with Windows by default and that most users never use, such as games, Clipchamp, and promotional apps.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will uninstall selected Microsoft pre-installed apps. Some of these can be reinstalled from the Microsoft Store if you change your mind. Review the list before proceeding.
commands.afterRunMessage: Microsoft bloatware has been removed. Your Start menu and app list should be cleaner.

aliases: fix an app that won't open
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Attempts to repair a selected application using its built-in repair function or the Windows app repair mechanism without losing your data or settings.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will attempt to repair the selected app. Your app data and settings should not be affected.
commands.afterRunMessage: The repair process has completed. Try opening the app again to confirm it is working.

commands.publicCommand: Reset an app to factory settings
aliases: app is acting weird reset it
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Resets a selected Microsoft Store app completely, wiping all its data, cache, and settings so it starts fresh as if newly installed.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will reset the selected app and permanently delete all its local data and settings. This cannot be undone.
commands.afterRunMessage: The app has been reset to factory defaults. Open it to go through its initial setup again.

aliases: use a different browser as default
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Changes your default web browser so that links from emails, documents, and other apps open in your preferred browser instead of Edge.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will change your default browser. Links from external apps will open in the new browser you select.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your default browser has been changed. Links and web content will now open in your chosen browser.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables app suggestions, promoted apps, and third-party app recommendations that appear in the Start menu and other parts of Windows.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable app suggestions and promotions in the Start menu and Windows interface. No apps will be installed or removed.
commands.afterRunMessage: App suggestions and promotions have been disabled. Windows will no longer advertise apps to you in the Start menu.

commands.publicCommand: Find apps I have never opened
aliases: what apps am I not using
find apps taking space doing nothing
see apps I forgot I had
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Identifies installed applications that have never been launched or have not been used in a long time based on last-run timestamps.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Checking app usage history. Nothing will be changed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Here are apps that appear to have never been launched or not used recently. Consider uninstalling any you truly do not need.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Resets and repairs the Microsoft Store app and its related components to fix issues with downloading, updating, or launching the Store.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will reset the Microsoft Store and clear its cache. You will stay logged in to your Microsoft account.
commands.afterRunMessage: The Microsoft Store has been reset. Try opening it again to confirm it is working.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Switches Windows and supported apps to dark mode, changing the system theme to use dark backgrounds and light text throughout the interface.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will switch Windows to dark mode. The change takes effect immediately for the system and most apps.
commands.afterRunMessage: Dark mode is now on. Some older apps may not fully support dark mode and will continue to display with a light theme.

commands.publicCommand: Fix blurry apps on my screen
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables the Windows automatic DPI fix for blurry apps and applies per-application DPI overrides so apps appear sharp on high-resolution displays.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will adjust DPI settings for blurry apps. Some apps may need to be restarted to apply the fix.
commands.afterRunMessage: DPI fix has been applied. Restart any blurry apps to see if they appear sharper now.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables Windows Night Light which reduces blue light from your display in the evenings to reduce eye strain and help you sleep better.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will turn on Night Light. Your screen colors will appear warmer. You can adjust the intensity or schedule in Display Settings.
commands.afterRunMessage: Night Light is now on. Your screen will use warmer colors to reduce eye strain. You can set a schedule so it turns on automatically at sunset.

commands.publicCommand: Set my monitor to its max refresh rate
aliases: monitor running at wrong refresh rate
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Detects your monitor's maximum supported refresh rate and sets Windows to use it, making motion smoother and more responsive on high refresh rate displays.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will change your monitor refresh rate. Your screen may flicker briefly as the setting is applied.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your monitor refresh rate has been set to the maximum supported value. Motion should appear smoother now.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Runs the ClearType text tuning wizard and adjusts font rendering settings to make text appear crisp and clear on your display.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will tune ClearType font rendering. You may be asked to choose the sharpest-looking text samples during the process.
commands.afterRunMessage: Font rendering has been tuned. Text should appear clearer across Windows and most applications.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Removes unnecessary icons, widgets, and system tray clutter from your taskbar to give you a cleaner and less distracting desktop.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will remove various taskbar elements including widgets, search bar, and unnecessary system tray icons. You can restore them later in Taskbar Settings.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your taskbar has been cleaned up. The most useful items remain and the clutter has been removed.

commands.publicCommand: Stop my screen from dimming on its own
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables adaptive brightness and content-based brightness adjustment so your screen stays at the brightness level you set it to.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will disable adaptive brightness. Your screen will stay at the brightness level you set manually and will not dim automatically based on content or ambient light.
commands.afterRunMessage: Adaptive brightness has been disabled. Your screen will no longer dim on its own.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables Windows HDR output on compatible monitors to display a wider range of colors and brightness for supported content.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will enable HDR on your display. Note that HDR can make some non-HDR content look washed out. Make sure your monitor and cable support HDR before enabling.
commands.afterRunMessage: HDR has been enabled. HDR content will now display with enhanced color and brightness. If non-HDR content looks washed out, you can adjust Auto HDR settings.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Increases the size and optionally changes the color of your mouse cursor to make it easier to see and track on screen.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will change your mouse cursor size. The change takes effect immediately.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your mouse cursor has been made larger. If you want to adjust it further, you can do so in Accessibility Settings under Mouse Pointer.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Diagnoses and fixes crackling or static audio by restarting the audio service and resetting driver settings.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will restart your audio service and apply driver fixes. Sound may cut out briefly during the process.
commands.afterRunMessage: If crackling persists, try switching to a different audio output device or updating your audio driver.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Opens the Windows volume mixer so you can set a different volume level for each running application.
commands.beforeRunMessage: The volume mixer panel will open. No system settings will be changed automatically.
commands.afterRunMessage: Drag each app's slider to your preferred level. Changes take effect immediately.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Raises the microphone input level and enables the microphone boost option in Windows audio settings.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will increase your microphone volume. This may introduce background noise at very high levels.
commands.afterRunMessage: Test your microphone in a call or recording app. Lower the boost if you hear hissing or distortion.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Restores missing audio by restarting the Windows Audio service and resetting the default playback device.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will restart the audio service. Sound will be interrupted for a few seconds.
commands.afterRunMessage: If sound is restored, you are done. If not, check that the correct output device is selected in sound settings.

commands.publicCommand: Stop Windows from lowering volume during calls
aliases: Volume too low during video call
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables the Windows communications audio ducking feature that automatically lowers other sounds during calls.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will disable automatic volume reduction during communication sessions.
commands.afterRunMessage: Other audio will no longer be reduced when you are on a call. Restart any open audio apps to apply the change.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Applies the known Windows registry fix for Bluetooth audio stuttering caused by Wi-Fi interference and driver conflicts.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will apply a registry change for Bluetooth audio. A restart is required for the fix to take full effect.
commands.afterRunMessage: Restart your PC and reconnect your Bluetooth device. Audio should no longer stutter.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Turns on file extensions in Windows Explorer so you can see the full filename including its type suffix.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will update your Explorer settings to always show file extensions.
commands.afterRunMessage: File extensions are now visible in Explorer. You can now tell .jpg from .png and .doc from .docx at a glance.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Configures Windows Explorer to display hidden and system files that are normally invisible.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will update Explorer settings to show hidden files. System files will become visible.
commands.afterRunMessage: Hidden files and folders are now visible. Be careful not to delete system files you do not recognize.

commands.publicCommand: Unlock a file that is in use
aliases: File being used by another program
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Identifies which process is locking a file and gives you the option to close that process to release the lock.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will scan for the process holding the file lock. Closing that process may affect running work.
commands.afterRunMessage: If the locking process was closed, the file should now be accessible. Try your operation again.

commands.publicCommand: Take ownership of a file I cannot access
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Transfers ownership of a locked or protected file or folder to your user account so you can access or modify it.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Taking ownership changes the file's security permissions. Only do this for files you own or are authorized to access.
commands.afterRunMessage: Ownership transferred. You should now be able to open, edit, or delete the file.

commands.publicCommand: Remove the 260 character file path limit
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables the Windows long path setting that lifts the 260-character file path limit for modern apps that support it.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will update a system policy to allow paths longer than 260 characters. A restart may be needed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Long path support is now enabled. Apps that support it can now use paths exceeding 260 characters.

aliases: What changed on my PC today
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists all files that were created or modified today so you can see recent activity across your PC.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will scan your user folders for files modified in the last 24 hours. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: Review the list. Unexpected changes may indicate background software or malware activity worth investigating.

commands.publicCommand: Create a folder shortcut that acts like the real thing
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Creates a symbolic link so a folder appears in two places at once without duplicating any data.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will prompt you for the source and target paths before creating the link.
commands.afterRunMessage: The symbolic link has been created. Programs that access the link will see the contents of the original folder.

commands.publicCommand: See the true size of a folder
aliases: How much space does folder use
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Calculates and displays the true disk size of a folder including all nested files and subdirectories.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will scan the folder and sum up all file sizes. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: The total size is shown above. Compare this against your drive free space to decide what to clean up.

commands.publicCommand: Restart my graphics driver without rebooting
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Sends the Windows display driver reset signal to recover from a frozen or crashed graphics driver without restarting.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Your screen will go dark for a few seconds during the graphics driver reset. This is normal and expected.
commands.afterRunMessage: If the display recovered, you are done. If it happened again soon after, consider updating or rolling back your GPU driver.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Pauses all Windows Update downloads and installs for 35 days to give you time to work without interruption.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will pause updates immediately. Remember to resume updates within 35 days to stay secure.
commands.afterRunMessage: Updates are paused for 35 days. Set a reminder to re-enable them before the pause expires.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Removes the most recently installed Windows update, which is useful when an update caused new problems.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will identify and remove the latest installed update. A restart is required to complete the process.
commands.afterRunMessage: The update has been removed. Pause updates to prevent it from reinstalling automatically.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Runs the Windows System File Checker to scan for and repair corrupted or missing protected system files.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will run SFC as an administrator. The scan can take 10 to 20 minutes. Do not close the window.
commands.afterRunMessage: If repairs were made, restart your PC. If SFC found errors it could not fix, run the deep repair option next.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Runs DISM to repair the Windows component store, which is required before SFC can fix certain deep corruptions.
commands.beforeRunMessage: DISM requires an internet connection and can take 20 to 30 minutes. Do not close the window or disconnect from the internet.
commands.afterRunMessage: After DISM completes, run the Repair corrupted Windows files command to apply the fixes.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Reads the SMART health data from your drives to give an early warning if a drive is showing signs of failure.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will query your drive's SMART data. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: A status of Caution or Failure means you should back up immediately and plan to replace the drive.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Schedules the Windows Memory Diagnostic to run on next restart and test your RAM for hardware errors.
commands.beforeRunMessage: The RAM test runs on your next restart and takes about 10 to 15 minutes. Save all your work before proceeding.
commands.afterRunMessage: Restart your PC now to begin the test. Results will appear after Windows loads back up.

commands.publicCommand: Show my PC crash and error history
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Opens the Windows Reliability Monitor showing a timeline of crashes, errors, and warnings to help identify patterns.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will open your reliability history. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: Look for repeating red X events. A pattern on specific days or after specific apps points to the cause.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Reads the Windows crash dump file and extracts the error code and driver name that caused the blue screen.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will read the most recent crash dump. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: The driver or error code shown is your starting point. Search for that driver name to find a known fix.

commands.publicCommand: Create a restore point right now
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Creates an immediate System Restore snapshot so you can roll back Windows settings if something goes wrong.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will create a restore point now. This takes about a minute and requires some free disk space.
commands.afterRunMessage: Restore point created. If something breaks later, use Roll Windows back to a restore point to recover.

commands.publicCommand: Roll Windows back to a restore point
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Rolls Windows back to a previous restore point, undoing recent system changes, driver installs, and app installs.
commands.beforeRunMessage: System Restore will revert Windows settings to an earlier point. Installed apps added after that point will be removed. Personal files are not affected.
commands.afterRunMessage: Restore complete. Your PC will restart. Check that the issue is resolved before reinstalling any apps.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Rebuilds the Windows boot record to fix a PC that fails to start or shows a missing operating system error.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This repair must run from the Windows Recovery Environment. SHLLSHOCKD will guide you through the steps.
commands.afterRunMessage: If the boot record was repaired, restart your PC normally. If the error persists, a deeper repair or reinstall may be needed.

commands.publicCommand: Remove a specific Windows update by KB number
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Removes a specific Windows update identified by its KB number, useful when a particular patch is known to cause problems.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will ask you for the KB number before proceeding. A restart is required after removal.
commands.afterRunMessage: The specified update has been removed. Pause updates to prevent it from reinstalling automatically.

commands.publicCommand: Record the steps to reproduce a problem
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Opens the Windows Steps Recorder to capture a screenshot and action log of everything you do to reproduce a problem.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Steps Recorder will open. Click Record, reproduce the problem, then click Stop. A report file will be saved.
commands.afterRunMessage: Share the saved report with support or IT so they can see exactly what happened step by step.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Stops the print spooler, clears all stuck jobs from the queue, and restarts printing so new jobs can process.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will stop the spooler, delete stuck jobs, and restart printing. All pending print jobs will be cancelled.
commands.afterRunMessage: The print queue is now clear. Resend any documents you still need to print.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Restarts the Windows Print Spooler service to recover from a spooler crash or frozen print system.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will stop and restart the spooler service. Printing will be unavailable for a few seconds.
commands.afterRunMessage: The spooler is running again. Try printing your document now.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Re-enables the Microsoft Print to PDF printer if it has disappeared from your list of available printers.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will restore the Print to PDF option in your printer list.
commands.afterRunMessage: Print to PDF is now available. Select it as your printer in any app to save a document as a PDF file.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists all installed printers including hidden ones and lets you remove printers for devices that are no longer connected.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will show all installed printers. You choose which ones to remove.
commands.afterRunMessage: Selected printers have been removed. Your printer list should now only show devices you actually have.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Discovers the IP address of your networked printer so you can access its web interface or configure it manually.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will scan your network and printer ports to identify the printer's IP address. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: The printer's IP address is shown above. You can use it to access the printer's settings page in your browser.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Sets a specific printer as your default and disables the Windows feature that automatically changes the default printer.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will show your installed printers and let you pick your default.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your default printer is now set and locked. Windows will no longer automatically switch it based on location.

commands.publicCommand: Open God Mode — every Windows setting in one place
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Creates and opens the God Mode folder that shows every Windows setting and control panel item in a single searchable list.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will create the God Mode folder on your Desktop and open it.
commands.afterRunMessage: The God Mode folder is now on your Desktop. Use the search box at the top to find any setting instantly.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables Windows clipboard history so you can press Win+V to see and paste from the last 25 things you copied.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will enable clipboard history. Press Win+V after this to see your clipboard history.
commands.afterRunMessage: Clipboard history is now on. Press Win+V anytime to pick from recently copied items.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables and opens Windows Sandbox, a disposable temporary Windows environment that resets completely when closed.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Windows Sandbox requires virtualization features. SHLLSHOCKD will enable it if not already on. A restart may be required.
commands.afterRunMessage: Sandbox is open. Install or test anything inside it — everything disappears when you close the window.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Opens the Windows screen recording tool so you can capture video of your screen with one keyboard shortcut.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will open the screen recorder. Press Win+Alt+R to start and stop recording.
commands.afterRunMessage: Your recording is saved to the Videos\Captures folder. Check there for your file.

commands.publicCommand: Start a focus session and block notifications
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables Windows Focus Assist to suppress notifications and pop-ups so you can work without interruptions.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will turn on Focus mode to silence notifications. You can turn it off at any time from the system tray.
commands.afterRunMessage: Focus mode is active. Notifications are silenced. Click the Focus icon in the system tray to end the session.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Opens the Windows emoji and special character picker so you can insert emoji anywhere you can type text.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will open the emoji picker. You can also open it anytime with Win+Period.
commands.afterRunMessage: Click any emoji to insert it. Use the search bar at the top to find specific emoji by name.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Activates Windows voice typing so you can dictate text into any text field by speaking into your microphone.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will enable voice typing. Press Win+H anywhere to start dictating.
commands.afterRunMessage: Voice typing is ready. Click in any text field and press Win+H to start speaking. Press it again to stop.

commands.publicCommand: Schedule automatic dark and light mode
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Sets up a scheduled task to automatically switch between light mode and dark mode at times you choose.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will ask for your preferred light mode and dark mode switch times before creating the schedule.
commands.afterRunMessage: The schedule is set. Windows will switch themes automatically at the times you specified each day.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables the Windows Nearby Sharing feature so you can wirelessly transfer files between Windows PCs nearby.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will turn on Nearby Sharing. Both PCs must have this enabled to send and receive files.
commands.afterRunMessage: Nearby Sharing is on. Right-click any file and select Share to send it to a nearby Windows PC.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Schedules your PC to shut down automatically after a specified number of minutes.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will ask how many minutes until shutdown before scheduling it.
commands.afterRunMessage: Shutdown is scheduled. Use Cancel a scheduled shutdown if you change your mind.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Cancels a previously scheduled shutdown so your PC stays on.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will cancel any pending shutdown timer immediately.
commands.afterRunMessage: Shutdown cancelled. Your PC will remain on until you shut it down manually.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables Windows Game Mode to prioritize CPU and GPU resources for your game and reduce background activity during play.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will enable Game Mode system-wide. It activates automatically when you launch a game.
commands.afterRunMessage: Game Mode is on. Your games should get more consistent frame rates and fewer background interruptions.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables the Xbox Game Bar background recording feature that can use CPU and disk resources while you play.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will disable background DVR recording. You can still record manually with Win+Alt+R.
commands.afterRunMessage: Background game recording is off. This frees up resources that were being used continuously during gameplay.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables Hardware Accelerated GPU Scheduling to reduce latency and improve frame times in compatible games.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will enable GPU scheduling. A restart is required for the change to take effect.
commands.afterRunMessage: GPU scheduling is enabled. Restart your PC and test your games for improved frame time consistency.

commands.publicCommand: Force an app to use the dedicated GPU
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Sets a specific application to always use your dedicated GPU instead of the low-power integrated graphics chip.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will ask which app to configure and then set it to high performance GPU mode.
commands.afterRunMessage: The selected app is now assigned to your dedicated GPU. Restart the app to apply the change.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables non-essential Xbox background services that run even when you are not gaming and consume system resources.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will disable Xbox telemetry and background services. Game Bar functionality may be reduced.
commands.afterRunMessage: Xbox background services are disabled. This frees RAM and CPU for games and other applications.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Enables Variable Refresh Rate support in Windows so compatible monitors can sync their refresh rate to your GPU output.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will enable VRR in Windows display settings. Your monitor must also support VRR (FreeSync or G-Sync).
commands.afterRunMessage: VRR is enabled. Screen tearing should be reduced or eliminated in supported games and apps.

commands.publicCommand: Sync my clock to the correct time
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Forces Windows to sync immediately with the internet time server to correct a clock showing the wrong time.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will force a time sync with the Windows time server. Your clock will be corrected in seconds.
commands.afterRunMessage: Clock synced. If the time drifts again quickly, your CMOS battery may need replacement.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Shows the history and status of Windows time synchronization including the last sync time and any sync errors.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will display your time sync history and current NTP status. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: If the last successful sync was days ago or shows errors, run Sync my clock to the correct time to fix it.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Opens the Windows region settings so you can change how dates, times, and numbers are formatted across the system.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will open the region format settings. You can change the format without changing your language.
commands.afterRunMessage: Date and time format updated. The new format will appear in Explorer, the taskbar clock, and most apps.

commands.publicCommand: Fix the clock after dual booting with Linux
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Fixes the clock conflict that happens when dual booting Windows and Linux by setting Windows to use UTC for the hardware clock.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will apply a registry change to how Windows reads the hardware clock. No restart is needed immediately.
commands.afterRunMessage: The fix is applied. Sync your clock once after this and the time should stay correct after switching between OS.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Opens Windows timezone settings so you can select the correct timezone for your current location.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will open the timezone selector. Picking the correct zone also corrects your system clock.
commands.afterRunMessage: Timezone updated. Your clock should now show the correct local time.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Configures Windows to boot into Safe Mode on the next restart, loading only essential drivers and services.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Your PC will restart and boot into Safe Mode. To exit Safe Mode, restart normally and it will not re-enter Safe Mode.
commands.afterRunMessage: Safe Mode is active. If your problem does not appear here, a third-party driver or software is the likely cause.

commands.publicCommand: Boot into the Windows recovery environment
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Restarts your PC directly into the Windows Recovery Environment for advanced repair and troubleshooting options.
commands.beforeRunMessage: Your PC will restart immediately into recovery mode. Save all open work before continuing.
commands.afterRunMessage: Select Troubleshoot from the recovery menu to access startup repair, restore points, and command prompt tools.

commands.publicCommand: Find what is slowing my startup
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Reads the Windows event log to measure how long each startup phase took and identify what is adding delay.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will analyze your last boot log. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: The longest phase or highest-delay startup item is your target. Use the startup manager to disable it.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Scans Device Manager for drivers flagged with errors or warnings so you know which ones need attention.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will scan Device Manager for problem devices and drivers. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: Any driver showing an error code needs to be updated or reinstalled. The error code tells you exactly what is wrong.

commands.publicCommand: Stop Windows from restarting automatically after a crash
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables the automatic restart after a blue screen so you can read the stop code before the PC reboots.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will disable auto-restart on crash. The next blue screen will stay on screen until you manually restart.
commands.afterRunMessage: Auto-restart is off. When you see the blue screen, note the stop code before restarting.

commands.publicCommand: Remove old entries from the boot menu
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists all entries in the boot menu and lets you remove outdated or orphaned entries from old OS installations.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will show all current boot entries. You choose which ones to remove — the active entry is protected.
commands.afterRunMessage: Selected boot entries have been removed. Your boot menu will be cleaner on next startup.

commands.publicCommand: Start Windows with only Microsoft services
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Disables all non-Microsoft services and startup apps so you can test whether a third-party program is causing your issue.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will configure a clean boot. Restart your PC after this to enter the minimal environment.
commands.afterRunMessage: If your problem is gone in clean boot, a third-party service is the cause. Re-enable services one by one to find it.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Displays a list of all currently running Windows services with their name, status, and startup type.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will list all running services. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: Review the list for unfamiliar services. Services you do not recognize are worth researching before disabling.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Permanently disables a specified Windows service so it no longer starts automatically or runs in the background.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will ask for the service name before disabling it. Disabling the wrong service can cause instability.
commands.afterRunMessage: Service disabled. It will no longer start on boot. You can re-enable it through Services manager if needed.

commands.publicCommand: Reset Windows but keep my files
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Reinstalls Windows while keeping your personal files, removing all installed applications and resetting system settings.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will reinstall Windows. All installed apps will be removed. Your personal files in Documents, Pictures, and Desktop will be kept.
commands.afterRunMessage: Windows has been reinstalled. Reinstall your applications and check that your files are intact.
commands.warningMessage: This will reinstall Windows and remove all installed applications. Your personal files will be kept but all apps must be reinstalled.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Performs a complete factory reset that erases all files, apps, and settings and reinstalls a fresh copy of Windows.
commands.beforeRunMessage: This will permanently erase everything on this PC including all files, apps, and accounts. Back up everything before proceeding.
commands.afterRunMessage: Factory reset complete. Windows is freshly installed. Set up the PC as if it were new.
commands.warningMessage: This will permanently erase all files, apps, and settings. This cannot be undone. Back up everything before proceeding.

commands.publicCommand: Create a Windows recovery USB drive
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Creates a bootable USB recovery drive that can start and repair Windows even if the PC cannot boot normally.
commands.beforeRunMessage: You need a USB drive with at least 16GB of free space. All data on the USB will be erased during this process.
commands.afterRunMessage: Recovery USB created. Store it somewhere safe. Use it to boot and repair your PC if Windows fails to start.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Immediately backs up your user files to an external drive or network location using Windows Backup.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will open Windows Backup. Connect your external drive before proceeding if backing up locally.
commands.afterRunMessage: Backup complete. Verify the backup destination contains your files before proceeding with any major changes.

commands.publicCommand: Roll back to the previous Windows version
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Uses the Windows Go Back feature to revert to the previous major version of Windows that was installed before the last upgrade.
commands.beforeRunMessage: The Go Back option is only available for 10 days after a major update. Your apps and files are preserved.
commands.afterRunMessage: Rollback complete. Pause updates to prevent the same upgrade from installing again automatically.

aliases: See what I can do to fix Windows
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists all available Windows recovery and repair options with an explanation of what each one does and when to use it.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will display all recovery options. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: Pick the least destructive option first. Work down the list only if the gentler options do not resolve the issue.

commands.publicCommand: Copy a large folder without crashing
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Uses robocopy to transfer a large folder reliably with automatic retry on errors and progress reporting.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will ask for the source and destination paths then begin the transfer. It will retry automatically if files are in use.
commands.afterRunMessage: Transfer complete. Check the summary log for any files that could not be copied.

commands.publicCommand: Sync a backup folder — copy only what changed
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Syncs a source folder to a destination by copying only new and changed files, making backups fast and efficient.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will ask for the source and destination paths. Only changed files will be copied. Nothing is deleted from the destination.
commands.afterRunMessage: Sync complete. The destination folder now matches the source for all new and changed files.

commands.publicCommand: Rename hundreds of files at once
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Renames all files in a folder using a pattern you define, such as adding a prefix, replacing text, or adding numbering.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will preview the renamed files before making any changes. You confirm before the rename is applied.
commands.afterRunMessage: Files renamed. The preview showed exactly what changed. Undo is available in Explorer if needed.

commands.publicCommand: Completely erase and format a drive
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Completely erases and reformats a selected drive, destroying all data on it permanently.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will ask you to confirm the drive letter twice before erasing anything. Verify the drive letter carefully.
commands.afterRunMessage: Drive wiped and formatted. It is ready for use. The previous data is unrecoverable.
commands.warningMessage: This will permanently destroy all data on the selected drive. This cannot be undone. Confirm the drive letter carefully.

commands.publicCommand: Show all connected drives including hidden ones
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Lists every connected disk and partition including drives without letters that are hidden from Explorer.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will query the disk subsystem. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: All connected drives are listed above. Drives without letters will not show up in Explorer until assigned one.

commands.publicCommand: Assign a drive letter to a drive that is not showing up
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Assigns an available drive letter to a connected disk that is online but not visible in Explorer.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will show unlettered drives and ask which letter to assign. The drive will appear in Explorer immediately after.
commands.afterRunMessage: Drive letter assigned. The drive should now appear in Explorer and be accessible normally.

commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Diagnoses why your laptop is overheating by checking CPU load, background processes, and power settings.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will run three checks to find the heat source. Nothing will be changed in this sequence.
commands.afterRunMessage: Review the findings above. The highest CPU or background process is most likely the heat source.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Investigates the most common causes of random freezes: failing RAM, a degrading drive, and recent error history.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will run three diagnostics. The RAM test requires a restart — you will be asked to confirm before that step.
commands.afterRunMessage: Review the results. A drive in Warning or Critical state or RAM errors found are the most actionable findings.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Stops the stuck update and optionally removes it so you can start fresh.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will pause all updates immediately. You will be asked before the update is removed.
commands.afterRunMessage: Updates are paused. If you removed the update, restart your PC. Re-enable updates within 35 days.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Resets the graphics driver and checks display settings to bring back a monitor Windows stopped detecting.
commands.beforeRunMessage: The screen will briefly go dark during the graphics driver reset. This is normal.
commands.afterRunMessage: If the monitor is still not detected after the reset, check the cable connection and try a different port.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Applies the known Windows fix for Bluetooth audio dropout and checks the Bluetooth service is running.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will apply the audio fix and check the Bluetooth service. A restart may be needed.
commands.afterRunMessage: If the device still will not pair, try removing it from Bluetooth settings and pairing fresh.

aliases: Slow to respond after turning on
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Runs the four most effective speed fixes in sequence: kills slow startup apps, stops background apps, frees RAM, and removes animations.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will show you what it plans to change before each step. You confirm each one.
commands.afterRunMessage: Restart your PC. The next boot should be noticeably faster.

aliases: How do I free up space
Help me free up disk space
commands.bookChapter: Where Did All My Space Go?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Runs four discovery scans to find the safest and largest space savings before deleting anything.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will scan for disk usage, large files, duplicates, and Windows junk. Nothing is deleted in this sequence.
commands.afterRunMessage: Review the findings. The scans tell you what exists — you decide what to remove.

commands.publicCommand: Why is the fan running so loud?
commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Identifies the process or app causing CPU/heat load that is making the fan run hard.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will check CPU and RAM load. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: The process using the most CPU is most likely the cause. Closing it should reduce fan noise.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Attempts to recover a deleted file by rolling Windows back to a restore point before the deletion.
commands.beforeRunMessage: System Restore will roll back Windows settings to an earlier point. Your personal files will be preserved, but installed apps may be affected.
commands.afterRunMessage: If restore completed, check for the file in its original location. If it is still missing, the file may not have been included in the restore point.

commands.bookChapter: Why Is This Thing So Slow?
commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Checks for pending restart flags and scheduled tasks that indicate whether a restart will help.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will check restart flags and scheduled tasks. No changes will be made.
commands.afterRunMessage: If a restart is pending, save your work and restart when ready. If not, the issue is likely not restart-related.

commands.plainEnglishPurpose: Reads crash history and recent error logs to identify the cause and suggests whether to repair or reinstall.
commands.beforeRunMessage: SHLLSHOCKD will check your reliability history and crash logs. No changes will be made in the first two steps.
commands.afterRunMessage: If the crash logs point to a specific file or driver, that is your starting point for the fix.
